# Chapter 7. The Close of the Capitalist Mode of Production

```{=html}
<p class="fst">We have reached the end of the exposition of the capitalist process of production which we have attempted with the guidance of Karl Marx.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>We have seen that the <em>primitive mode of production</em> rests upon social, systematically organised labour, and requires that the means of production and products be <em>social property</em>. The products are indeed distributed and thereby become individual property, but only in so far as they are <em>objects of use</em> for individuals. As the immediate yield of social labour, the products first of all fall to society.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>This mode of production is displaced by the <em>simple commodity production</em> of private workers acting independently of one another, each of whom produces, with means of production belonging to himself, products which then, as a matter of course, are also his private property.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But out of simple commodity production develops <em>capitalist commodity production</em>. In place of the individual workers producing independently of one another step large, concentrated productive establishments, each independent of the others and producing commodities, but each also internally organised for systematic social production. Since these large capitalist establishments confront one another as commodity producers, the exchange of commodities, and with it the property right of simple commodity production, remains in force in their mutual intercourse: private property in the means of production and products.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But with this, private property too has been turned into its opposite.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Under simple commodity production, private property was the consequence and fruit of labour. The worker was the owner of his means of production and his products. Capitalist production tears apart the connection between labour and property. The worker no longer has any property in his product. On the contrary, means of production and products belong to the non-worker. The transformation of production into a social one on a capitalist basis increasingly accomplishes the transformation of the non-workers into owners of all wealth, and of the workers into the propertyless.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>With this, the contradiction between the prevailing mode of production and the prevailing mode of appropriation is not yet exhausted.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>We have seen how simple and transparent production took shape under primitive communism, how society directed it according to its will and its needs.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Under the system of commodity production, the social conditions of production become a power that grows over the head of the individual producer. He becomes its will-less slave, and his position becomes all the more wretched because the new masters do not prescribe his tasks to him, do not communicate their needs to him, but leave it to him to guess them. Production is now subject to laws which operate independently of the producers, and often even against their will, like natural laws; laws which assert themselves through the periodic occurrence of abnormal conditions, such as falling prices, dearness, and so forth. Yet these abnormalities, in so far as they spring from social causes, remain slight and confined to narrow spheres under the rule of simple commodity production, corresponding to the low productivity of the fragmented establishments of individual workers.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Now the productivity of labour is enormously heightened by the capitalist mode of production, which unleashes and causes to grow colossally all those productive forces peculiar to social, purposefully organised labour, and which takes into its service the natural forces subjugated by science. The consequence is that the periodic occurrence of abnormal conditions, through which the laws of commodity production assert themselves and which formerly brought in their train only transient, local inconveniences that could easily be got over and often also averted, now takes shape as periodic catastrophes which last for years, afflict whole empires and continents, and wreak the most frightful devastation; periodic catastrophes which grow in extent and intensity with the capitalist mode of production, and which now seem to be running out into a chronic wasting sickness.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>And yet one more thing: under primitive communism, where the product of social labour belongs to society and is distributed by it to the individuals in accordance with social needs, the share of each grows with the growth of the productivity of labour.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Under the rule of commodity production, the mass of use-values corresponding to a given magnitude of value grows with the productivity of labour. Under simple commodity production, the product of his labour as a rule belongs to the worker. He can consume it wholly or partly himself: in this case the quantity of objects of use at his disposal evidently grows in the same measure as the productiveness of his labour. But he can also exchange the product of his labour wholly or partly — under simple commodity production, only a small part of the product becomes a commodity.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>For the product of a given labour which he exchanges, he will receive the more use-values the greater the productivity of labour in general. Here too the growth in the productiveness of labour accrues undiminished to the worker's benefit.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Under capitalist commodity production, labour-power itself is a commodity, whose value, like that of every commodity, falls in the measure in which the productivity of labour rises. The greater, therefore, the productivity of labour, the smaller the proportionate share in its advantages that the worker receives in the price of labour-power. But the more the capitalist mode of production becomes the predominant one, the more the mass of the people consists of wage labourers, and the more, therefore, it remains excluded from the fruits of the heightened productivity of its labour.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>All these antagonisms necessarily produce out of themselves conflicts between the capitalist class and the workers, conflicts which awaken the latter to class consciousness, drive them to political activity, and call forth workers' parties in all capitalist countries. But the circumstances just touched upon also produce sufferings of the most manifold kind, and not only such as are confined to the working class — sufferings which make the present conditions appear intolerable to ever wider circles even outside the class of wage labourers.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Thus everything presses towards a resolution of the contradiction embodied in the capitalist mode of production, the contradiction between the social character of labour and the inherited form of appropriation of the means of production and the products.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Only two ways seem possible to resolve it; both amount to bringing the mode of production and the mode of appropriation into harmony with one another. The one way leads to the abolition of the social character of labour, to a return to simple commodity production, to the replacement of the large-scale enterprise by handicraft and small-peasant agriculture. The other way does not seek to adapt production to the mode of appropriation, but the mode of appropriation to production; it leads to social property in the means of production and the products.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Many today attempt to force the course of development onto the first way; they proceed from the erroneous view that the mode of production can be shaped at will by legal regulations. Bourgeois vulgar economics, the advocate of capital, condemns these attempts — where it has not become altogether debased.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But it itself attempts a similar game. In order to make the ruling mode of production appear in harmony with the ruling mode of appropriation, it disregards in its economic expositions the peculiar and essential characteristics of the modern mode of production and presents it as though it were simple commodity production: one need only read the current writings of the vulgar economists: there commodities are still exchanged today as among barbarians, there hunters and fishers, who dispose freely of forest and sea, appear as wage labourers, bow and arrow, boat and net as capital. <a href="#ab3-kap7-n1" id="ab3-kap7-f1" name="ab3-kap7-f1">[1]</a> Marx, in his <strong>Capital</strong>, thoroughly finished off this sort of economist.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But his work accomplished still more than merely to expose vulgar economics in all its shallowness and untruth.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>People are fond of calling Marx the spirit that ever denied, who was able only to dissolve things critically, but not to work positively.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Yet even the present outline of the exposition of the production process of capital which Marx has given us may show that he has in fact created a new economic and historical system. The critique of his predecessors forms only its foundation.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>One cannot overcome the old without having oneself climbed to a higher standpoint beyond it; one cannot criticise without having acquired a higher knowledge; one cannot tear down a scientific system without having erected behind it another, grander and more comprehensive one.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Marx was the first to lay bare the fetish character of the commodity, who recognised capital not as a thing but as a <em>relation mediated by things</em>, and as a <em>historical category</em>. He was the first to investigate the laws of the movement and development of capital. And he was the first to derive the goals of the present social movement as necessary consequences of the historical development up to now, instead of constructing them in his head at his pleasure as demands of some kind of "eternal justice."</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>From the standpoint to which Marx raises us, one recognises not only that all the attempts of the vulgar economists to falsify the present conditions into patriarchally simple ones are just as futile as those to reshape them back into such conditions.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>One also recognises the only way that remains for the further development of society: the adaptation of the form of appropriation to the mode of production, the taking possession of the means of production by society, the complete, unreserved carrying-through of the transformation of production from individual production into social production which capital has only half carried through. But with this a new epoch begins for humanity. In place of anarchic commodity production comes the planful, conscious organisation of social production; the domination of the product over the producer comes to an end. Man, who has to an ever-increasing degree become master of the forces of nature, thereby becomes also master of social development. "Only from then on will men make their own history with full consciousness," says Engels, "only from then on will the social causes set in motion by them have, predominantly and to an ever-increasing degree, also the effects willed by them. <em>It is the leap of humanity from the realm of necessity into the realm of freedom."</em></p>
```

## Notes

```{=html}
<p class="note"><a href="#ab3-kap7-f1" id="ab3-kap7-n1" name="ab3-kap7-n1">1.</a> The illusions which these gentlemen seek to arouse are brought to nought in the <em>colonies</em>, that is, in those with virgin soil which are colonised by immigrants. There we find full freedom of the labour contract, the property of the worker in his products, hence in the yield of his labour; there we find in general the conditions which our economists present as those of the capitalist mode of production: but strangely enough, under these conditions capital ceases to be capital!</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="note">In such colonies free land is still available in abundance, and access to it stands open to all. There every worker can as a rule produce independently; he is not compelled to sell his labour-power. In consequence, everyone prefers to work for himself rather than for others. With this, money, means of subsistence, machines, and other means of production cease to be capital. They do not valorise themselves.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="note">The same economists who in the capitalist countries declaim so pathetically about the sanctity of property and the freedom of the labour contract therefore demand, in young colonies, in order that capital may flourish there, the exclusion of the workers from landed property and the promotion of their immigration by the state or at the expense of the earlier-arrived workers themselves — in other words, the forcible separation of the worker from the means of production and subsistence and the artificial creation of a surplus worker population which is in fact not free, but compelled to sell its labour-power. And where a pliant working class — especially if of a backward race — is at hand, to whom this may be offered, the undisguised forced labour, slavery, is proclaimed.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="quoteb">“The same interest which, in the mother country, compels the sycophant of capital, the political economist, to declare the capitalist mode of production theoretically to be its own opposite, this same interest drives him here (in the colonies) <em>to make a clear breast of it</em> (to confess everything openly) and loudly to proclaim the antagonism of the two modes of production.”</p>
```


---

[↑ Previous: Chapter 6. The Dawn of the Capitalist Mode of Production](ab3-kap6.qmd) [↑ Return to Table of Contents](../index.qmd)
